Important: Please Save Your Own Copy Before Editing :) 



SEVEN FIGURE EMAILS #7 

The “Sarcastic Sally” (aka the Deadpool)



Ry Schwartz & Copy Hackers

2020



SEVEN FIGURE EMAILS #7

About The “Sarcastic Sally”

The Persuasion Deep Dive

VIP Writing Tips:

When to send it:

When NOT to send it:

Message Mining Q’s:

Themeplate A (10xEM Example)

Themeplate B (10xFC Example)

See it in action:

10x Emails Mastery 2017/2018 (Themeplate A)

10x Freelance Copywriter 2018 (Themeplate B)

About The “Sarcastic Sally” 

We rarely rarely rarely re-use email copy across multiple launches or funnels -- but this one was used as a closing email in both 10xEM17 and 10xEM 18 --- both of which brought in north of 125K in a short, 4-day launch window (without a ton of pre-launch build up or webinars) to support it. 



At its core, this template leverages sarcasm as a framing device for the sales arguments that may not have landed with enough impact earlier in your launch. Repackaging your testimonials, your benefits, your objection-handling within the frame of sarcasm can help cut through white noise and make sure those elements are READ and ABSORBED with full attentiveness.



While some people despise sarcasm, you can’t deny its influence in pop culture. Bill Murray pulls it off (while remaining mostly endearing).. Chandler Bing from Friends has a whole offshoot youtube series showcasing his best “sarcastic” moments, and sarcasm is a major comedic style used in both Deadpool films (which combined have grossed $1.5B worldwide). 



It’s also one of the easier comedic styles to pull off in your copy -- and one your audience is most likely to understand (unlike high brow humor that can fail to land) 



Before we get into it, I will say this: The “Sarcastic Sally” is a more advanced sales template that requires full buy-in to pull off. Meaning, If you’re gonna write it -- write it. Keep the sarcastic frame going right till the end -- and don’t waver in your delivery/confidence. 



The Persuasion Deep Dive

Persuasion Principle #1: Pattern Interrupt 



The main reason this email works is because it cuts through the white noise and delivers your most critical sales arguments within a new (and entertaining) frame. 



It’s about getting those sales arguments seen/felt/absorbed more deeply by using a frame that commands attention. 



Persuasion Principle #2: Preempting Objections (aka objection aikido) 



Almost every product/service suffers from an industry-wide objection (ie. nobody finishes online courses, realtors aren’t worth their cut, X is dead/doesn’t work anymore, etc.). By getting ahead of the objection and using its own momentum to your benefit, you perform a type of “objection aikido” that not only disarms it, but strengthens your position (while displaying confidence). 



Persuasion Principle #3: Playfully positioning against alternatives 



Not only can you pre-empt objections, but you can use the sarcastic framing to position against pretty much anything. (that includes different product categories/alternatives or even your prospect’s “status quo” / inaction) 











VIP Writing Tips: 

If you’re gonna write the Sarcastic Sally -- maintain the sarcastic frame right until the end. If you cherry-pick where you use sarcasm (as most do)... it becomes confusing and harder to decipher where you’re being sarcastic and where you’re being sincere. 



When being sarcastic -- especially in self-deprecating you/your product -- and “lifting’ up alternatives -- err on the side of exaggeration (or being a bit more absurd) so that you reader is absolutely clear that you’re being sarcastic. 



The opening should be self-deprecating (which gives you greater permission to make your competitors/alternatives) the subject of the sarcasm later. If you came right out of the gates using sarcasm against a competitor… it can come off as mean-spirited and even unlikeable/obnoxious.



Extra credit: 

How to be sarcastic without being a jerk (also delves into using hyper-specificity and other techniques that bring your copy to life). 

When to send it:

In the final 72 hours of a launch / evergreen funnel - after your prospect has already seen your pitch in a “normal” way. 

When NOT to send it:

As a “launch day” email

As a stand-alone (without other sales emails in the sequence) 

Message Mining Q’s:

What’s one major objection my prospect has about my product, product category, or industry as a whole? 

ie. realtors aren’t worth it. 

ie. Email is dead

Ie. tax attorneys are overpriced 



What proof do I have (testimonials/case studies) that reinforce my position / run counter the primary objection? 



What are the “alternatives” they’d consider using instead? How can I playfully/sarcastically position against that? 

Ie. self-listing

A/I, bots, etc.

doing your own taxes



Themeplate A (10xEM Example)

[Sarcastic, Self-Deprecating Opener] 

[State the so-called “alternatives”] 

[Maintain sarcastic frame]

[MOHP - sarcastically framed as a negative] 

[Contrast with how the alternative would look - framed as a positive] 

[Testimonials -- written sarcastically] 

[Break the frame by listing the big benefits/promise] 

[The “play it safe” PS - argue against the misconception you opened with] 

Themeplate B (10xFC Example)

[Battlefield: do you want the pain/MOHT?]

[Sarcastically offer the path towards the MOHT]

[Sarcastically frame the MOHT itself as desirable]

[Break frame -- transition to those who want to escape that pain]

[Exclusive Empowerment]

[CTA] 

[Final close that provides contrast to your opening] 

[Optional PS. Bullets to support your webinar/product] 



See it in action: 

10x Emails Mastery 2017/2018 (Themeplate A)

Subject:

Email is dead. 

RIP email. 



Body:

Bet you heard email was dead.

Murdered by messenger bots.

Killed by AI.

Replaced by telepathy.

And it's like, Good riddance, right? Email was soooooo bad.

The WORST.

I mean, with email, you have to -- get this -- you have to sit down, open ConvertKit or Intercom or whatever, and type out messages to groups of people who've signed up to hear from you... and then you have to wait, like, sometimes whole minutes for those first notifications from PayPal and Stripe: Payment received.

Ugh. Gross.

Messenger bots - now THERE'S tech I can get behind.

And AI - I mean, that's the future. Sure, it's still far in the future. But, y'know, it'll get there. And when that happens, you'll just create a card in Trello called "Activate AI to do campaign" and, voila! Ain't nothing artificial about those results. I mean, unless you count that they don't actually exist yet.

But back on topic: email is the worst.

It took this one copywriter named Prerna Malik TWO emails to sell out almost twenty thousand dollars worth of productized services. Yes, two. Like, why not ONE, right?

Ugh. Email.

And Marian Shembari only made half a million dollars for Teachable with the emails she wrote for them. I mean, how are you supposed to be valued at a billion dollars by this time next year if you keep chipping away at things half-millions at a time?

But I guess if that's your thing.

If you LIKE the value proposition of directly accessing a list of prospects any time of day with any offer you're promoting - using tech you've already got access to and don't need to hire a 19 year-old to explain to you - then k, cool, sure maybe email's not dead for you.

In which case you should join this before 4pm PST today

~jo :)

PS: Oh, email's not dead for you? Cool. Yeah, it seems to me, my clients and the people asking me for referrals to great copywriters that email is, in fact, anything but a member of the White Walker Army.

It’s alive.

It’s kicking.

And it’s dropping extra zeroes into the Stripe accounts of the startup founders, growth marketers, online entrepreneurs and talented copywriters who know how to tap into its conversion power.

Email has survived a thousand "next big things" coming and going. You're reading email right now. And whether you hated this email or loved it, you're reading it.

You've got subscribers who will read your emails, too.

Do you know what to write in them?

Do you know HOW to write them?

Now's the time to conquer email copywriting - even if you're not a copywriter.

Email is a channel that is not going anywhere. And email copywriting is a skillset you'll be able to leverage at every turn in your business.

Learn to write email copy that converts

It's all inside 10x Emails Mastery, which closes in:



Just make sure to act now before rigor mortis sets in and we slide this offer into the Copy Hackers freezer.

Join us in 10x Emails Mastery here



10x Freelance Copywriter 2018 (Themeplate B) 

Subject:

The expert’s guide to building a mediocre AF freelance career.

The ultimate guide to building a career you hate



Body:



Wanna have the world’s most mediocre freelance copywriting career?



 It’s easy.



… Hang up a shingle (not that painful rash that old people get).  



… Roll out of bed at 11am. Work in your Sponge Bob slippers and maintain an inner sense of satisfaction that you’ve transcended the matrix and live a better life than all your troglodyte friends who are still slogging it out for “the man”. 



… Change your linkedin handle to John “the amazing copywriter” Doe. Bonus points if you lie about your success and get your second cousin Luke to write a recommendation on how you totally “crushed” the copy for his tire-changing business. Do it. No one will ever know. 



Wanna get to the land of mediocrity even faster? 



Keep making these 4 cringe-worthy mistakes that are stunting your freelance copywriting business. 



See? Mediocrity is easy. 



And the rewards are awesome…



1. You get to complain! (sometimes in gif form)

2. You get to secretly hate the copywriters at the top (damn illuminati)

3. You get to do easy and safe things that never trigger a single droplet of cortisol until your brakes need fixing and you realize you’ve only billed enough to cover your rent and groceries and Luke isn’t willing to do you a solid. 



If that’s all you want out of the #freelancelife, read no further.



Pull up a snuggie and continue Netflix’ing your way into “good enough”. 



But if you’ve armed yourself with some deft copywriting skills, have some real wins under your belt and want to see just how far you can take your freelance business in 2019…



… then I borderline insist that you save your seat for this one.



Being a freelance copywriter is not just another part of the “gig economy”.



It’s one of the most valuable and  lucrative skills on the planet.



A cool six-figures and the swagger to walk into any room (or Zoom call) with kanye level confidence is built within the DNA of freelance copywriting.



Chances are, you just keep getting its double helix all twisted up in self-doubt and silly mistakes that continue to sabotage your ascent into freelance glory. 



Join me here to unknot those DNA strands and see what your career can grow into this year. 



-jo



PS.  You need bullets? We got bullets.



Join moi this Tuesday to finally get real answers to: 



Why so few prospects bother responding to your proposals (and how to PRESENT a proposal)

The competitive advantage you're soooo close to having in your grasp

How the world's best-paid copywriters attract incredible clients (no, it’s not ‘cos we’re special little snowflakes)

The 1 thing that's separating you from the promise of a rewarding freelancing life

Why you've been spinning your wheels with this whole freelance game - and how to get a grip on earning well (so your family actually acknowledges that you’re running a real business that pays real bills and puts real shoes on real feet)



If that’s not worth your 55 mins, I hear there’s a new season of Narcos playing on Netflix. Not as good as the first 3 (sans Pablo Escobar)... but good’enuf to keep you in the land of good’enuf.



For my more ambitious freelance folk… 



Here be that link one more time
